name: Setup
goal: Bootstrap Hedl from nothing to a self-hosting framework: a deterministic completion gate, an adversarial review system, tiered skill distribution, and the work-tracking machinery — all installed into Hedl's own repo so the project develops under its own discipline.
definition_of_done: The deterministic completion gate (.github/scripts/am_i_done.py) exists and runs locally, exiting 0 only when every applicable check passes. (Gate execution in CI was broken by symlinked workflow files throughout Phase 0 and was repaired early in Phase 1 via WORK-0030.)
The Hedl skill is installable into a consumer repo via skill/hedl/scripts/install.py across all three tiers (gate, lightweight, team).
The core slash commands exist in .claude/commands/ and are routed from skill/hedl/SKILL.md.
The adversarial review system exists: named reviewer agents in .claude/agents/ plus the composable reference library, selected by .work/config/dispatch-rules.json.
The governance foundation is recorded as ADRs in .work/decisions/.
The work-tracking machinery exists and is seeded: .work/work.json, .work/context.json, and .work/phases/.
constraints: am_i_done.py must remain deterministic — no inference in the gate (ADR-003).
Distribution is an Agent Skill installed by projection, not a fork (ADR-001).
Repo history stays linear and pristine (ADR-006).
Existing pytest suite must pass throughout.
learning_goals: Can a deterministic gate plus adversarial review be packaged as a single installable skill with tiered, opt-in adoption?
Does Hedl dogfooding itself (adopting via install.py --tier team) surface contradictions the design missed?
assumptions_to_validate: The framework can dogfood itself — Hedl's own development runs under Hedl's gate and phase discipline.
Process artefacts (ADRs, commands, agents) stay proportionate to shipped capability.
